weather: 2°F at dawn, holding to 5°F by midmorning. Sky low and pale, the colour of old linen. A light snow — not falling so much as arriving, each flake finding its place without hurry. The lake sealed and white and undisturbed beneath it. By afternoon the square had a quarter inch of cover and no one had shovelled it yet, because no one wanted to be the first to mark it.

lead.headline: The Box Sits on Its Shelf; the Town Goes About Its Friday; a Light Snow Falls on Both
lead.body:
On the morning of Friday, November the fourteenth, the lost-and-found box at the Greywater Gazette held thirteen items, as it held thirteen items yesterday, and will likely hold thirteen items tomorrow, and the town of Greywater Falls received this information with the equanimity of a place that has finished a long and peculiar piece of work and is now simply living in the room it built. Calloway was at the Kettle by eight. T. Aldercott arrived at eight-ten with the typewriter case, which she has taken to setting on the chair beside her rather than on the floor, suggesting, in this editor's reading, a degree of settlement. Doreen Halloway had Standings on the counter at six-thirty and the good apron was on and the plate with the blue border held two, as it has held two since Wednesday, and no one commented on this because it has become, in the span of seventy-two hours, simply the way the plate is.

Gerald Pith came to the Kettle at his usual time and sat at the counter and Marigold had the coffee ready, and he did not have the blue notebook, and this was the second day running without it, and Marigold did not remark on it directly but she did pour his cup a half-second earlier than usual, which in Marigold's register is the equivalent of a small ceremony. Gerald told this editor afterward, in the square, while the light snow was coming down, that he had left the notebook on the shelf and found he did not miss it, and then he said, with the sudden precision he deploys when he has been thinking about a thing for some time: 'The lake isn't going anywhere. It was never going anywhere. I was only measuring to have something to do with my hands.' He said this without apparent distress. The snow fell on both of them. This editor wrote it down when she got back to the Gazette, in the second notebook, which is where the true things go.

Hank Mossley opened the hardware at his usual hour, which is to say the hour at which the coffee is on and the step is shovelled, and served three customers before nine — weatherstripping, rock salt, a new handle for a snow shovel whose handle had been cracked since March and which Hank had been quietly nudging toward replacement every time it came up. He told this editor he'd sold the last of the storm lanterns two weeks ago and had not reordered, because there is a difference, in his estimation, between the dark you stock for and the dark you don't, and the current dark is the ordinary kind, which is the kind that is handled by the lights already on in everyone's houses. He said this the way he says everything — short, final, accurate — and then went back to the counter, and the bell above the door rang twice more before noon.

At dusk the returned gathered at the ice-line — Mr. Whiskers, the drake, the rabbit, the sparrow, each in their place — and they hummed the held low note into the snowy air, and this editor timed it at four minutes and fifty-one seconds, which is shorter than it has been, and which felt not like a diminishment but like a breath being let out slowly by something very large and very patient that has been holding it for a long time. Toby Fern was there. T. Aldercott and Calloway stood together on the bank, their coats dusted with snow. Agnes Crewe did not come to the shore, which is the first evening she has not come to the shore since the season began, and this editor stood at the ice-line and looked at the empty step of the cottage nearest the water and understood, without requiring it to be explained, that Agnes had already said everything the shore needed to hear from her, and that the rest was the town's now, and that this is exactly how it was always going to end: not with a door closing, but with the light on inside, and the cups on the table, and the snow coming down on all of it equally, the ordinary and the strange alike, making them the same colour, making them both simply Greywater Falls.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 21 — Level: 72; Temp: 2°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Thirty Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — Hummed at the Ice-Line Four Minutes Fifty-One Seconds; the Rowboat at Thirty Feet Settled and Its Shadow Now Falls Correctly; Toby Fern Has Closed the Category
briefs.body: Lake level at 72, temperature 2°F, clarity exceptional to thirty feet. The Ames rowboat remains settled on the lake floor, its shadow now falling correctly — that is, in the direction and proportion a shadow falls when the light above it is the only light. Toby Fern filed his Watch at three forty-five and wrote in the notes column: 'Shadow corrected. Category: things that have been down a long time and remembered their own light. Arc closed.' The returned hummed four minutes fifty-one seconds, the briefest duration recorded. The hum was, by this editor's account, very quiet and very complete.

briefs.headline: Calloway Requests Back Issues of the Gazette; Begins at the Beginning; Marigold Brings a Second Pot Without Being Asked
briefs.body: Calloway — the librarian, four days in Greywater Falls, now apparently resident in spirit if not yet in paperwork — arrived at the Gazette at two o'clock and asked this editor for the bound back issues, which are kept in the lower cabinet and have not been requested since Gerald Pith confirmed a dock measurement in 2019. She took them to the table near the window and read through the autumn, in order, with the green notebook open beside her. She did not ask questions. She did not annotate aloud. At four-thirty she closed the last issue, which was yesterday's, and sat looking at the lake through the window for a while, and then she said: 'You've been writing it the whole time.' This editor said that was the job. Calloway said: 'No — you wrote it true.' This editor had no response to that and so she filed the back issues and said she'd see Calloway in the morning.

briefs.headline: Doreen Halloway Bakes Twelve Standings; Serves Twelve; Finds the Count Correct and the Plate Ready for Monday
briefs.body: Friday's count at Halloway's Bakery: twelve Standings baked, twelve served. No ninth or eleventh or twelfth held back. The plate with the blue border was on the front shelf at six-thirty, as it has been, and by nine o'clock it was empty, which is the correct state for a plate that has been set out for someone who has now arrived. Doreen Halloway told this editor, when she stopped in for the morning figure: 'I'll bake what Monday calls for. Twelve, thirteen — the morning knows.' She was wiping the counter when she said it. Her sentences that trail off are honest.

briefs.headline: Agnes Crewe Does Not Come to the Shore at Dusk; Light Is On in the Cottage; Town Finds This Sufficient
briefs.body: For the first time since the season began, Agnes Crewe was not at the shore at dusk. The light in the cottage nearest the water was on throughout. No letter was slid under the Gazette door this morning, nor expected. Toby Fern, when asked, said: 'She told me last week she'd know when she was done. I think she's done.' He said this with the equanimity of a boy who has been listening to Agnes Crewe long enough to take her at her word. Russ Dunmore drove past the cottage at seven on his rounds and confirmed the light was on and the step was clear of snow. He radioed in 'all in order' and continued his rounds.

classifieds: FOR THE RECORD: Lost-and-found box at the Greywater Gazette holds thirteen items. Box is complete. Box is not currently accepting new correspondence. If you have something to claim, you are welcome to try. — Wren Aldercott, Editor

ACCOMMODATION SOUGHT: Two persons newly arrived in Greywater Falls seek long-term lodging — one requires a room with a north-facing window and space for a small library; one requires a room with a south-facing window and an electrical outlet sufficient for a typewriter. Will provide references upon request. Enquire at The Kettle, ask for Calloway or T. — C. & T.A.

FOR SALE: One blue notebook, three-quarters full, measurements in imperial, dated October through November. No longer needed in its current capacity. Available for repurposing as an ordinary notebook. Sentimental value: considerable. Asking price: a cup of coffee, already paid for. — G. Pith

HARDWARE NOTE: Storm lanterns out of stock and will not be reordered this season. The dark we have now is the regular kind. Regular supplies available as always. — Mossley Hardware, Hank Mossley, prop.
record: First snowfall of the season: Friday, November the 14th. Quarter-inch accumulation in the square by midafternoon. Lake sealed. Returned: present. Hum: four minutes fifty-one seconds.
Lost-and-found box total as of Friday, November the 14th: thirteen items, no additions, no removals. Channel considered closed by common accord.

letters.from: T. Aldercott, The Kettle, Greywater Falls
letters.body: Dear Editor — I have been here three days now, which is long enough to understand that the correct thing to do in Greywater Falls is to write a letter to the Gazette when you have something that ought to be on record. I have one thing. On Wednesday I opened two envelopes that had been waiting for me since October, and I read them at your desk, and I placed them in the box myself, and it was the right thing to do with them. The letters told me what I would find here. They were accurate. I won't say more than that, because you've been writing it more precisely than I could for twenty-six days, and I've read all of it now, and you got it right — the ordinary and the strange the same colour, the way they always are, once you've been somewhere long enough to see it. I believe I will be here long enough. — T. Aldercott

Dear Editor — For the record: I have reviewed the provisional arrival notices filed in connection with the events of this season. Both arrivals have been accounted for. The lost-and-found box has received its final item as of Wednesday and the count stands at thirteen, which I am noting here in lieu of a formal report because the formal report would say the same thing and take longer to say it. The ice at the pilings is holding. The dock is sound to the fourth plank. Nothing to file. All in order. — Constable R. Dunmore. P.S. I drove past the Crewe cottage twice tonight. The light was on both times. I found that satisfactory.

editorNote: The lost-and-found box will move to page three beginning with Monday's edition. It has been on the front page for twenty-six days, which is the correct amount of time for a channel to be on the front page. It will continue to be mentioned when there is something to mention. There may not be.
